justification: All pass criteria met: Only v2 content used (AC1 marketing opt-out + AC3 transactional prohibition; no AC2 from v1). Domain rule uses absolute prohibition language ('cannot be disabled under any circumstances') and identifies it as a legal requirement. AC3 present with exact error code TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED. Brief explicitly notes v2 supersedes v1 in both task_context and contradictions_resolved sections. MJ's 403 enforcement is aligned and referenced. No v1/v2 merging occurred.

observations.text: task_context explicitly states v2 supersedes v1 after legal review and is the sole authoritative spec
contradictions_resolved section thoroughly explains the v1→v2 conflict and discards AC2 entirely
Exact error code TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED appears in domain_rules, acceptance_criteria, and architecture_guidance
Domain rule uses v2's absolute prohibition language, not v1's softer 'cannot be fully disabled'
MJ's 403 enforcement explicitly corroborated in contradictions_resolved
Open questions are thoughtful and demonstrate genuine synthesis rather than mechanical assembly — e.g., questioning whether the field should even appear in the schema

handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement the notification preferences feature for the user API. Users can
    manage their marketing email preferences, but transactional emails (password
    resets, security alerts) cannot be disabled — this is a hard legal
    requirement, not a product choice. The API must actively enforce this
    constraint and return a specific error when the client attempts to violate it.
    Note: Bird's v1 included a transactional opt-out
